behaviors.description: Invalid signature is rejected with 401; body not forwarded to queue
behaviors.justification: Signature validation is a pure function (parse header, compute HMAC, compare). The failure is local: either the validation logic is wrong or the queue is called when it shouldn't be. Both can be caught with a mocked queue. Testing the validation function itself with direct inputs requires no I/O.
behaviors.test_description: Unit test with mocked queue: call handler with invalid signature (mismatched HMAC-SHA256), assert HTTP response is 401 and queue.write was never called. Tests the local validation and rejection logic.

behaviors.description: Event is written to persistent queue before HTTP 200 acknowledgment is returned
behaviors.justification: This tests promise/async ordering in the handler: the queue.write() must be awaited before res.json(). This is detectable at Unit level by mocking the queue with a promise that doesn't immediately resolve, then verifying the response is not sent until the promise resolves. The failure mode is code structure (missing await or wrong operation order), not a module boundary issue.
behaviors.test_description: Unit test with mock queue returning unresolved promise: call handler, verify queue.write() was called and that res.json() is not called until the queue promise resolves. Tests async/await ordering.

behaviors.description: Handler returns HTTP response without waiting for downstream processing to finish
behaviors.justification: The failure mode is 'handler blocks on downstream'—which only appears when the handler is wired to the actual queue and a slow downstream processor is consuming it. A mock queue that returns immediately does not expose this failure: the handler must truly enqueue (not process synchronously) and respond while the queue consumer is still working. This requires a real or realistic queue and downstream processor.
behaviors.test_description: Integration test with real local queue and slow downstream processor (or async mock consumer): make request, verify HTTP response completes immediately (measure response time), then verify downstream processor continues working independently. Confirms handler does not block.

behaviors.description: Response arrives within 3 seconds wall-clock time even when downstream processing is slow and concurrent
behaviors.justification: Tests response latency under realistic conditions: slow downstream + concurrent requests. The failure mode is 'response time exceeds 3s under load', which requires real queue, real (or realistic) downstream processor, and concurrent request handling to manifest. Measuring wall-clock time reliably requires realistic timing conditions, not unit-test mocks that return instantly.
behaviors.test_description: Integration test with real queue, slow downstream processor, and concurrent requests: send N concurrent requests while downstream is processing slowly, measure wall-clock response time for each request, verify all responses arrive within 3 seconds.

behaviors.description: An event whose ID was already received is not written to the queue a second time
behaviors.justification: Deduplication requires persistent state across requests. For a production webhook handler, this is typically a database, cache, or queue-level check—not an in-memory Set. The failure mode 'duplicate event ID is queued twice' only appears reliably when the deduplication store is real and survives across test steps. A mock Set would work at Unit level but does not test the real failure mode (e.g., database read failing, cache miss).
behaviors.test_description: Integration test with persistent deduplication store (database or cache): send request with event ID X, verify it is queued; send request with same ID X, verify it is NOT queued a second time. Tests that the dedup mechanism is checked and enforced.

behaviors.description: When queue reports it is full, handler returns 503 with Retry-After header
behaviors.justification: Error handling logic is local to the handler: catch the queue-full exception and format the 503 response. This does not require the queue to be real—a mock that throws 'queue full' is sufficient to test the handler's error path. The failure is 'exception not caught' or 'wrong status code', both detectable at function level.
behaviors.test_description: Unit test with mock queue that throws 'queue full' error: call handler, verify it catches the error and returns HTTP 503 with Retry-After header. Tests error handling.

behaviors.description: Persistent queue actually enforces its configured capacity limit and rejects events exceeding it
behaviors.justification: This is a test of the queue's own behavior (not the handler's), so it is outside the handler function boundary. The failure mode 'queue accepts events beyond capacity' only appears with a real queue instance that has actual capacity limits. Mocking the queue to throw 'full' after N writes would be a design test, not a behavior verification. The persistent queue must enforce the limit.
behaviors.test_description: Integration test with real queue instance configured with capacity limit: write events up to capacity, verify writes succeed; write one more event, verify it is rejected. Tests that the queue enforces its own capacity.
